title: Butterfly symmetrical papercut
subtitle: Cutting a folded sheet to produce a symmetrical butterfly silhouette
excerpt: A symmetrical papercut is made by folding the paper before cutting: every cut made on one half is mirrored automatically when the paper is unfolded. The butterfly is an ideal first design because its natural bilateral symmetry and simple wing outline give a satisfying result with very few cuts.
sourceNotes: Traditional folk papercut traditions (scherenschnitte, jianzhi, wycinanki) as documented in nineteenth-century craft literature. The bilateral symmetry technique is the foundational method common to all European and East Asian hand-papercut traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: A design property in which the left and right halves are mirror images. In papercut work, bilateral symmetry is produced by folding the paper along the centre axis before cutting, so that every cut is simultaneously made on both halves.
The technique of folding paper before cutting to produce symmetrical or repeated shapes. The fold acts as the axis of symmetry; unfolding the cut reveals the mirror image.
The areas removed by cutting, which define the shape of the positive silhouette. In papercut design, the eye reads the negative space as the outline of the subject.

content.text: to produce both wings simultaneously from a single folded sheet. The
cut away from the wing defines its shape: the silhouette is the paper that remains.

items.name: Coloured craft paper, A5, one sheet
items.notes: 80 to 120 gsm. Heavier paper gives a stiffer result; tissue weight (20 gsm) gives a delicate, translucent butterfly but is harder to cut cleanly.

items.name: Pencil
items.notes: For drawing the half-butterfly template on the folded paper before cutting.

items.name: Scissors
items.notes: Small pointed scissors cut curves more cleanly than large craft scissors.

items.name: Scalpel (optional)
items.notes: For cutting interior negative shapes such as the spots on the wings.

content.text: Fold and draw the half design

Fold the A5 sheet in half lengthways (portrait, fold on the left). The fold line is the body axis of the butterfly. On the open right half, draw one wing: a forewing curving up and out from the fold, a hindwing curving down and out below it. Keep the wing attached to the fold at the body: do not cut all the way to the fold, or the butterfly will separate into two unattached wings. Draw spots or interior cuts within the wing area if desired.

Cut along the pencil outline through both layers of the folded paper. Cut the outer wing outline with scissors. For interior spots or cut-out shapes, use the scalpel and cutting mat with the paper still folded. Work from the smallest interior cuts first, then cut the outer outline last. This order prevents the paper from flexing or tearing while the outer silhouette is still intact.

Open the folded cut carefully: the two halves are mirror images. Lay the butterfly face-down and press the fold line flat with the back of a fingernail or a bone folder. Place under a heavy book for ten minutes if the fold line is stubborn.
